pattern: Records can arrive out of timestamp order and can be corrected later, so track the authoritative price per timestamp in a map, and answer max/min with lazily-cleaned heaps that discard any popped entry whose price no longer matches the map's current record for that timestamp.
hints: Keep a hashmap from timestamp to its current (possibly corrected) price, plus track the single largest timestamp ever seen so 'current' is a direct lookup.

For maximum/minimum you need fast access to the best price among only the LIVE (uncorrected) records, but a plain running max/min can go stale once a correction lowers or raises a price. A max-heap and a min-heap of (price, timestamp) pairs handle new prices in O(log n), but old, now-incorrect entries can linger in the heaps.

Push every update's (price, timestamp) onto both heaps without ever removing the old entry for that timestamp (that would cost O(n)). When reading the heap's top for maximum/minimum, first check the map: if the timestamp's currently recorded price doesn't match the heap entry's price, that entry is stale — pop it and keep checking the new top until one agrees with the map.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: two updates, a correction to an earlier timestamp, then current/maximum/minimum queries
correcting the timestamp that currently holds the max down to a low value must lower the reported maximum, not leave a stale heap entry winning
correcting the timestamp that currently holds the min up to a high value must raise the reported minimum
two different timestamps sharing the same price: correcting one of them must not affect the other's contribution to max/min
current() reflects the price at the largest timestamp recorded, even when it was updated long before later, smaller timestamps arrive
a single update satisfies current/maximum/minimum all agreeing on the one price
repeated corrections to the same timestamp in a row must always resolve to only the latest one

stress.description: many updates over a small pool of timestamps (forcing frequent corrections) interleaved with current/maximum/minimum queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    ops = ['StockPrice']
    args = [[]]
    ops.append('update'); args.append([1, rng.randint(1, 1000000000)])
    ts_pool = list(range(1, 51))
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        c = rng.choice(['update', 'update', 'current', 'maximum', 'minimum'])
        if c == 'update':
            ts = rng.choice(ts_pool)
            price = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
            ops.append('update'); args.append([ts, price])
        else:
            ops.append(c); args.append([])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: a small timestamp pool (1..50) guarantees frequent same-timestamp corrections, which is exactly what stresses lazy heap invalidation
